top_weaknesses.issue: Several slogan/aphorism lines drift the voice from analytic-reportorial into vibe-y commentary: 'The cyberpunk reading is exact', 'This isn't a bug in the model. It IS the model', 'a higher-stakes arms race on the same field'
top_weaknesses.fix: Either tether each slogan to a concrete mechanism in the next sentence, or cut the slogan and let the FTC evidence carry the weight

top_weaknesses.issue: Lede leads thesis-first (perfect price discrimination → checkout flow); lacks one concrete checkout-screenshot anecdote to ground the abstraction before the reader hits the FTC citations
top_weaknesses.fix: Open with a specific real example (the $42 sweater anecdote later in the piece could be promoted into the lede)

top_weaknesses.issue: Anchor text leads with publishers/institutions ('2025 review by Snell & Wilmer', 'antimonopoly analysis of AI') instead of query-shaped topic phrases
top_weaknesses.fix: Rewrite anchors topic-first: 'FTC surveillance pricing 6(b) study (Jan 2025)', 'Yale Law & Policy Review: antimonopoly approach to governing AI'

top_weaknesses.issue: The 'forward question' section drifts into speculative futurism (buyer-side agents) without anchoring to anything emerging today
top_weaknesses.fix: Add one bridging sentence naming a real surveillance-pricing case (Wendy's dynamic pricing rollout, Uber surge, Kroger digital tags) before forecasting

consensus_summary: Sharpest closing question of the batch and clean H2 outline; bottlenecked by slogan-style lines that pop credibility and a thesis-first lede that needs a concrete checkout anecdote up top. Tighten the slogans, ground the lede, ship.
